content.text: Step-back prompting trades one extra LLM call for higher factual accuracy by retrieving an explicit principle before answering. The trade-off is only favourable when the question actually depends on a retrievable principle — one that lives in the model's training knowledge and, if stated correctly, constrains the specific answer. For many common task types the extra call adds latency and cost without improving accuracy. Understanding when the pattern helps (and when it does not) lets you apply it precisely.

Ask one question: does answering this correctly require recalling a general principle or conceptual framework before reasoning about the specific case? If yes, step-back helps. If the bottleneck is sequential computation (arithmetic chains, logic puzzles) or pure memorised facts (dates, names), step-back does not help — use chain-of-thought or a direct call instead.
Physical laws, chemical reactions, and biological mechanisms are principle-governed
Retrieve the correct formula first, then compute step-by-step
Broader context improves causal attribution; less reliable for exact dates
Bottleneck is sequential arithmetic, not principle recall
Simple fact lookup (birth year, capital city)
No principle to abstract; answer is a memorised fact
Bottleneck is search / deduction depth, not knowledge recall
No principle constrains the answer; abstraction adds no accuracy
A simple programmatic classifier routes questions to the right strategy. In production, this routing can itself be a lightweight LLM call with a structured output schema.
The question asks "why" or "how" about a natural or social phenomenon — these are almost always principle-governed.
You can describe a plausible general question that is one level more abstract than the specific question and that has a clean factual answer.
The model has previously produced a wrong or inconsistent answer to similar questions — suggesting the bottleneck is principle recall, not computation.
The question belongs to a domain with well-defined laws: chemistry, physics, biology, economics, formal logic applied to real-world contexts.
Accuracy matters more than latency; you can afford the second LLM call.
Signals That Step-Back Will Not Help
The question asks for a single memorised fact: a name, date, or definition that the model retrieves directly from training.
You cannot write a useful abstract question — the only abstraction is "what is the answer to this question?" which collapses back to the original.
The task is creative, subjective, or open-ended: writing, brainstorming, summarisation. There is no principle that constrains the output.
The bottleneck is computation (long arithmetic, constraint propagation) rather than knowledge — adding a principle to context does not fix arithmetic errors.
Latency or cost is the binding constraint and accuracy is already acceptable with a direct call.

import anthropic
import time

client = anthropic.Anthropic()


def timed_call(prompt: str, max_tokens: int = 300) -> tuple[str, float, int]:
    start = time.monotonic()
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=max_tokens,
        temperature=0.0,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    elapsed = time.monotonic() - start
    tokens = response.usage.input_tokens + response.usage.output_tokens
    return response.content[0].text.strip(), elapsed, tokens


question = "Why does copper turn green when exposed to air and moisture over time?"
abstract_q = "What chemical reaction occurs when copper is exposed to oxygen and moisture?"

# Direct approach
direct_ans, t1, tok1 = timed_call(question)

# Step-back approach
principle, t2a, tok2a = timed_call(
    f"Answer this general question in 2 sentences:\n{abstract_q}"
)
grounded_ans, t2b, tok2b = timed_call(
    f"Principle: {principle}\n\nUsing this principle, answer concisely:\n{question}"
)

print(f"Direct:     {t1:.2f}s, {tok1} tokens")
print(f"Step-back:  {t2a + t2b:.2f}s, {tok2a + tok2b} tokens (2 calls)")
print(f"Overhead:   {(t2a + t2b - t1):.2f}s extra, {tok2a + tok2b - tok1} extra tokens")
print()
print(f"Direct answer:     {direct_ans[:200]}")
print(f"Grounded answer:   {grounded_ans[:200]}")
Real-time chat assistant, latency < 1 s target
Two-call overhead is user-visible; embed abstraction in a single prompt
Latency does not matter; principled grounding reduces error rate
Combine grounded retrieval with majority vote across N samples
Token cost per call is low; accuracy gain on weaker models is proportionally larger

The clearest diagnostic: if you can write a meaningful abstract question that has a concise, accurate factual answer independent of the specific case, step-back will help. If the only generalisation of the question is trivially the question itself, skip it. The extra call pays off only when the retrieved principle genuinely constrains the final answer — otherwise it is overhead with no benefit.
